coredata.dc:title: Tunable band structure of synthesized carbon dots modified graphitic carbon nitride/bismuth oxychlorobromide heterojunction for photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline in water

coredata.dc:description: In this study, graphitic carbon nitride (CN) decorated with carbon quantum dot (CQD) and bismuth oxychlorobromide (BiOClxBr1-x) was fabricated by calcination and hydrothermal methods. The morphology characterization of the synthesized photocatalyst revealed that CQD and BiOClxBr1-x solid solution were deposited on the CN surface. CQD served as the electron reservoir, which could reduce the recombination of electron-hole pairs, thus improving the overall photocatalytic performance. The synergistic effect of 1 wt% CQDs and BiOCl0.75Br0.25 markedly improved the interfacial charge transfer efficiency and light-harvesting capacity of the composite. The degradation rate of tetracycline (TC) over CN/CQD/BiOCl0.75Br0.25 was 83.4 % after 30 min and favorable stability with near-initial capacity under visible light irradiation. Meanwhile, the reaction mechanism of the photocatalytic performance was demonstrated by the analysis of the surface adsorption sites, efficient utilization of visible light, and charge carrier transfer. The degradation by-products and potential degradation pathways were also analyzed using liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry. Finally, the toxicity estimation software tool (T.E.S.T) analysis indicated that the toxicity of most intermediates was lower than TC. This work provideed a strategy for fabricating visible light (VL) photocatalyst with excellent photocatalytic activity, furnishing a new insight for interface charge transfer.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, graphitic carbon nitride (CN) decorated with carbon quantum dot (CQD) and bismuth oxychlorobromide (BiOClxBr1-x) was fabricated by calcination and hydrothermal methods. The morphology characterization of the synthesized photocatalyst revealed that CQD and BiOClxBr1-x solid solution were deposited on the CN surface. CQD served as the electron reservoir, which could reduce the recombination of electron-hole pairs, thus improving the overall photocatalytic performance. The synergistic effect of 1 wt% CQDs and BiOCl0.75Br0.25 markedly improved the interfacial charge transfer efficiency and light-harvesting capacity of the composite. The degradation rate of tetracycline (TC) over CN/CQD/BiOCl0.75Br0.25 was 83.4 % after 30 min and favorable stability with near-initial capacity under visible light irradiation. Meanwhile, the reaction mechanism of the photocatalytic performance was demonstrated by the analysis of the surface adsorption sites, efficient utilization of visible light, and charge carrier transfer. The degradation by-products and potential degradation pathways were also analyzed using liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry. Finally, the toxicity estimation software tool (T.E.S.T) analysis indicated that the toxicity of most intermediates was lower than TC. This work provideed a strategy for fabricating visible light (VL) photocatalyst with excellent photocatalytic activity, furnishing a new insight for interface charge transfer.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Water pollution has been caused by contaminants such as phenol, antibiotics, nitrides, and various organic pesticides, increasing concerns as industrialization proceeds. Antibiotics are among the most widely used pharmaceutical compounds in medicine, veterinary, and aquaculture to prevent and treat microbial diseases [1]. Upon ingestion, digestion, and metabolization of antibiotics in the body, residual antibiotics and metabolites are excreted into the environment via urine and feces, resulting in severe environmental contamination [2]. Different technologies have been used to remove and degrade antibiotics, including filtration, surface adsorption, ozonation, ion exchange, and biodegradation [3,4]. These techniques are costly, inefficient, and most of the time lead to secondary pollution. Unlike the conventional methods, the photocatalytic system is a well-established and green technique with cost-effective, high-performance, and environmentally sustainable alternative for the degradation of antibiotics in water [5].

However, the thermodynamic contradiction between optical absorption and redox potentials has made a single-component photocatalytic system challenging for antibiotic degradation [6,7]. Inspired by natural photosynthesis, systems consisting of two different photocatalysts are desirable for achieving overall degradation efficiency [8]. The overall process is separated into two steps i.e. holes oxidize water molecules at a more positive valence band (VB) to form hydroxyl radical (•OH) while the photogenerated electrons in the more negative conduction band (CB) reduce oxygen to make superoxide (O2 •-). At the two photocatalyst interfaces, holes at a more negative valance band recombine with electrons at a more positive conduction band. Such Z-scheme configurations broaden the choice of photocatalytic systems for the degradation process, as semiconductor materials can provide the required redox potential.

In this context, metal-free graphitic carbon nitride (CN) with an optical bandgap of 2.7 eV, adequate conduction, and valence bands, is suitable for harvesting visible light with a wavelength less than 460 nm to suppress organic pollutants [9–11]. However, the rapid recombination of charge carriers and low specific surface area restrict its photocatalytic activity [12–15]. To overcome such drawbacks, different techniques have been employed to improve the photocatalytic activity of CN, such as element doping [15,16], depositing with noble metals [17], coupling with co-catalysts [18], modifying structural morphology [19], and constructing heterojunction [20,21]. Remarkably, carbon quantum dots (CQD) have dimensions ranging from 2 to 10 nm garnered considerable attention because of their high up-conversion capacity and excellent electron transfer properties. They can accelerate the kinetics of surface reactions and prevent charge pair recombination in CN [16,22]. Meanwhile, the conjugated л structure of CQD enhances the interactivities between CQD and CN, resulting in a practical approach for achieving targeted photocatalytic performances. For instance, Hong et al. [23] and Jian et al. [24] found that introducing the CQDs onto CN fostered the degradation of organic pollutants by forming additional reactive oxygen species. In another study, Xie et al. reported that 0.5 % of CQDs caused the highest TC degradation efficiency, which was 3.5 and 46.2 times higher than that of MoO3/CN and CN, respectively [10]. The enhanced photocatalytic performance was ascribed to the synergistic effects of CQD properties (i.e., excellent up-converted photoluminescence (PL), high charge separation capacity, y and the Z-scheme heterojunction structure). However, an opportunity to promote the quantum efficiency and overall mineralization ability of CN/CQD lies in efficient compositing semiconductors with a high oxidation ability.

Bismuth oxyhalides (BiOX (X = Cl, Br, I)) compounds have emerged as potential candidates for photocatalytic applications [25,26]. These compounds exhibit a lower bandgap as halogen atomic number increases, utilizing efficient visible light absorption [27]. Because of this feature, BiOX compounds can be designed by mixing halogens intentionally to improve their basic characteristic and photocatalytic performance. For example, GaN and ZnO as individuals only absorb UV light, whereas (Ga1−xZnx)(N1−xOx) solid solutions utilize the visible light region and demonstrate outstanding photocatalytic activity for water splitting [28,29]. The BiOX1Y1-X based heterojunction materials showed better photocatalytic performance in comparison with bare BiOX photocatalyst [30,31]. Keller et al. observed no quaternary phases in BiOX1−xYx (X, Y = Cl, Br, and I) solid solutions, BiOCl1−xBrx and BiOBr1−xIx form systems of unlimited mutual solubility, whereas BiOCl1−xIx has limited solubility when the amount of iodine is higher [30]. Ren et al. found that optimized BiOX1−xYx solid solutions possessed higher photocatalytic activity than pure BiOX compounds because of the wider range of the visible light response and the reduced recombination rate of electron-hole pairs [32]. Therefore, the development of a highly active CN/CQD-based homojunction, combined with the BiOClxBr1-x solid solutions, is of great scientific and industrial importance.

Herein, we describe a strategy for loading BiOClxBr1-x solid solution and CQDs onto CN sheets to modulate the energy band structure and form an up-converted PL system. The obtained CN/CQD/BiOClxBr1-x ternary composite was investigated to photo-catalytically degraded tetracycline (TC) under visible light illumination. The ternary composite’s structures, components, and optical properties were investigated via characterizations. The effects of various factors, including the CQDs content, halogen loading, pH, organic–inorganic ions, and the actual wastewater on photocatalytic activity, were investigated. In addition, the possible photocatalytic mechanism was explored. The degradation pathway for TC was proposed based on the detection of intermediates through liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS). Finally, the toxicity of these intermediates was evaluated by quantitative structure–activity relationship (QSAR) prediction.

Melamine (C3N6H6), bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O), ethylenediamine (C2H8N2), potassium chloride (KCl), potassium bromide (KBr), potassium nitrate (KNO3), magnesium sulfate (MgSO4), isopropyl alcohol (IPA), potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7), 1,4-benzoquinone (C6H4O2), sodium azide (NaN3), triethanolamine (C3H15NO3), ethanol (C2H5OH), ethylene glycol (CH2OH)2, humic acid (C9H9NO6), potassium dihydrogen phosphate anhydrous (KH2PO4), hydrochloric acid (HCl), sodium carbonate (NaCO3), were all purchased from Sigma–Aldrich and used as received.
The CN was prepared in the following: 3.0 g melamine was placed in an alumina crucible and heated to 550 °C at 3 °C/min for 3 h. The obtained bulk CN was ground into powder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQD and CN/CQDs photocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQD) were synthesized by a hydrothermal method. The detailed experimental process was listed in the supporting information. The CN/CQD preparation process was as follows: a certain quantity of CQD was dissolved in 30 mL of deionized water. Then, 3.0 g of melamine was added to the above solution. The mixture was stirred at 70 °C to remove the solvent. Subsequently, the product was heated to 550 °C for 3 h at a heating rate of 3 °C/min. After being cooled to room temperature, the resultant yellow powder was collected. Different CN/CQD samples with CQD at mass percentages of 0.5 %, 1.0 %, 1.5 %, and 2.0 % were prepared through the same procedure.

The hydrothermal method was used to prepare CN/CQD/BiOClxBr1-x heterojunctions (“x” represents the chlorine content in halogen). First, 0.2 g of CN/CQD was added to 30 mL ethylene glycol and dispersed with an ultrasonic bath for 15 min before adding 1.0 mmol Bi (NO3)3·5H2O The mixture was then magnetically stirred for 30 min, and the resulting solution was designated as solution A. The halogen precursors KCl and KBr (a total of 1.0 mmol) were dissolved in distilled water (30 mL) with stirring to form solution B. Solution A and solution B were gradually mixed. After 30 min of vigorous stirring, the homogeneous suspension was transferred to Teflon-lined stainless steel for an 8-hour treatment at 160 °C. Once the product cooled to room temperature, the precipitation was collected and centrifuged. The impurities were removed by repeated washing with distilled water and ethanol. Lastly, the resultant products were dried at 60 °C. For comparison, CN/CQD/BiOCl photocatalysts were prepared under the same conditions without adding the Br precursor.

The phase structures of the prepared photocatalysts were measured by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer) with CuKa radiation. The surface morphology and elemental mapping of the photocatalyst were confirmed by scanning electron microscope (Quanta Feg 450) outfitted with an energy dispersive X-ray (EDX). Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images and high-resolution transmission electron microscopic (HRTEM) images were carried out on a JEM-2100F field emission electron microscope at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded by Bruker Tensor 27 Spectrophotometer in the region 4,000–500 cm−1. The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were obtained by Multi Technique X-ray Spectrometer (Axis Ultra DLD XPS, Kratos). The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were measured on a Perkin Elmer UV–vis spectrophotometer, Lambda 35. The Brunauer Emmett Teller (BET) specific surface area of the as-prepared samples was determined by ASAP 2010 (micrometrics, U.S.A.). Barrett Joyner Halenda (BJH) model measured the pore size distribution. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were demonstrated on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7000, Hitachi, Japan). Total organic carbon (TOC) was analyzed by TOC measuring instrument (Shimadzu TOC-5000).

Electrochemical impedance measurements were conducted using a Bio-Logic SP-300 potentiostat in a typical three-electrode cell system with sample coated FTO glass substrate as the working electrode (2 cm2 sample area), Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode, and platinum wire as the counter electrode in dark and illuminated conditions. The photocatalyst samples were dispersed in ethanol and dropped to the FTO glass substrate. The impedance measurements were carried out in the frequency range of 106 kHz to 0.01 Hz with 10 mV sinusoidal perturbation. Photocurrent responses with time in the presence and absence of light were measured by CHI660C electrochemical workstation with approximately 30 s time interval of light on and off. In both studies, 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution was used as the supporting electrolyte.

The photocatalytic performance of the synthesized photocatalysts was evaluated by decomposing tetracycline (TC) under visible light irradiation. A 500 W Xe lamp with a cut-off filter (>400 nm) was used as the light source. 100 mg of the synthesized photocatalysts were dispersed in 200 mL containing TC solution (10 mg/L) in a typical photodegradation system. The solution mentioned above was continually stirred in the dark for 60 min before irradiation to achieve absorption desorption equilibrium between the photocatalysts and TC molecules. After that, every 30 min, 5 mL of the reaction suspension was withdrawn. The concentration of TC was determined by UV–vis spectrophotometer at the absorption peaks of 357 nm. Four repeated cycle experiments with the same photocatalyst were conducted to determine the reusability. In each set of reactions, the photocatalyst was centrifugated and washed with distilled water and ethanol to remove the residual pollutants and then dried under an oven at 60 °C for further use.

The photocatalytic studies with the addition of radical scavengers, except for the control reaction conditions, were carried out to analyze the reactive oxygen species (ROS) generated in photocatalytic reactions. Triethanolamine (TEOA), isopropyl alcohol (IPA), 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ), potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7), and sodium nitrate NaN3 were used as quenchers of h+, •OH, •O2 –, e, -1O2, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Crystal structure, morphology, and chemical composition analyses
ce:section.ce:para: The XRD analysis was applied to evaluate the crystalline structure of CN, CN/CQD, CN/CQD/BiOCl, and CN/CQD/BiOClxBr1-x composites with different halogen content, as shown in Fig. 1 . The pure CN and CN/CQD show similar distinct diffraction peaks. Similar diffraction peaks can be ascribed to CQDs' low content, which XRD cannot resolve [33]. The strong diffraction peak at 27.08° could be assigned to the (002) interplanar, and the distance is 0.326 nm, and the other weak peak at 13.17° corresponds to the (100), which is the in-plane structure of tri-triazine units of 0.672 nm. XRD ensures that the pure CN sample has a hexagonal phase and is consistent with the (JCPDS No 87–1526). Meanwhile, the diffraction peaks of CN/CQD/BiOClxBr1-x samples with × = 1.0 were identical to the conventional diffraction patterns of BiOCl's tetragonal phase (JCPDS No.06–0249). The addition of Br to the CN/CQD/BiOCl crystal increased the angle of the CN/CQD/BiOClxBr1-x diffraction peaks because the ionic radius of Br- (0.196 nm) is larger than that of Cl- (0.181 nm), which indicate the formation of solid solutions.

SEM images of the CN, CN/CQD, CN/CQD/BiOCl, and CN/CQD/BiOCl0.75Br0.25 composite were shown in Fig. 2 . The CN displays irregular plate-led morphology (Fig. 2a), while CN/CQD shows the presence of black dots on the CN (Fig. 2b). Moreover, the CN/CQD/BiOCl and CN/CQD/BiOCl0.75Br0.25 composite, the white plate of BiOCl0.75Br0.25 are well embedded on the surface of CN/CQD (Fig. 2c and d). This efficient bonding indicated the successful construction of the composite. The Edx mapping image demonstrated that all the elements (i.e., C, N, O, Bi, Cl, Br) uniformly distributed over CN/CQD/BiOCl0.75Br0.25 composite and the absence of foreign atoms that may appear as an impurity Fig. 2e.

To better understand the morphology, the detailed morphology of CN, CN/CQD, CN/CQD/BiOCl, and CN/CQD/BiOCl0.75Br0.25 nanocomposite was further investigated by TEM (Fig. 3 a-d). The morphology of the pure CN (Fig. 3a) appears a two-dimensional sheet-like structure with a smooth surface. The two-dimensional sheet-like structures can also be observed in the CQD/CN sample, except that appearing some black dots on their surfaces (Fig. 3b). The TEM images of the composite indicate that CN sheets are covered with the CQD and BiOCl0.75Br0.25 nanoplates (Fig. 3c-d). The HRTEM technique was also used to perform additional microscopic characterization, as shown in Fig. 3e. The HRTEM image displays regular lattice planes of 0.326 nm and 0.277 nm, corresponding to the CN/CQD plane (002) and the tetragonal BiOCl0.75Br0.25 plane (011), respectively [34]. In addition, in Fig. 3e, a contact boundary between CN/CQD and BiOCl0.75Br0.25 can be seen, which is presumed to form an interfacial heterojunction. The excellent coupling of CN/CQD and BiOCl0.75Br0.25 allowed for charge transmission and separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, which led to increased photocatalytic activity.

The chemical structures of CN, CN/CQD, CN/CQD/BiOCl, and CN/CQD/BiOClxBr1-x heterojunctions were further analyzed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). Fig. 4 shows the FT-IR spectrum of the as-prepared samples. The peak at 807 cm−1 is the distinctive absorption peak of the triazine ring, and the various peak bands at 1,240–1,630 cm−1 are the CN and CN rings of heterocyclic stretching vibration [10,11]. It was observed that the peaks in the CN/CQD composite exhibited a slight redshift compared to pure CN, which was attributed to the weakening of the CN bond due to the strong interaction between CN and CQD. In the case of CN/CQD/BiOClxBr1-x composite, a series of peaks in the range of 1,240–1,630 cm−1 belonging to CN/CQD are easily observed, while the peaks at 2,350 and ∼ 3,500 cm−1 of the BiOClxBr1-x are assigned to the OH stretching and bending vibrations, respectively. The small band centered at 1,044 cm−1 can be designated as Bi-X (X = Cl, Br) band symmetry stretching vibration peak. No characteristic absorption peaks of the ionic liquid are found in the FT-IR spectrum, showing that the ionic liquid can be easily removed from the surface of the photocatalyst by washing with water and alcohol. Both XRD and FTIR results indicated that the CN/CQD/BiOClxBr1-x composites were successfully prepared.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to determine the elemental composition and chemical states of as-prepared CN/CQD, and CN/CQD/BiOCl0.75Br0.25 photocatalysts. The XPS survey spectrum revealed the presence of C, N, and O for both photocatalysts, whereas Bi, Cl, and Br, only appear for CN/CQD/BiOCl0.75Br0.25 (Fig. 5 a). The contents (atomic %) of the element and the surface atomic ratio of the prepared samples are listed in Table S1. The N:C ratio of the CN/CQD/BiOCl0.75Br0.25 (1.362) was lower than that of the CN/CQD (2.15), suggesting the introduction of nitrogen vacancies [35]. Moreover, due to the presence of Bi-O in CN/CQD/BiOCl0.75Br0.25, the O:C ratio of CN/CQD (0.09) increased to 0.27. The atomic ratio of Cl: Br was theoretically 3:1. However, experimentally, it was found to be 2.4:1, which demonstrated the presence of Cl and Br in the composite material. The high-resolution XPS spectra of Bi 4f, O1s, C1s, N1s, Cl 2d, and Br 3d are shown in Fig. 5b-g. The peaks at 159.7 eV and 164.8 eV in Fig. 5b are assigned to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2, respectively. This demonstrates that the presence of inter-layered Bi3+ in the BiOClxBr1-x does not affect the CN/CQD/BiOCl0.75Br0.25 heterostructures [36]. The spectrum of O 1 s in Fig. 5c, in addition to the two peaks associated with CO (533.7 eV) and C-OH (535.7 eV), also provides a new broad peak at 531.8 eV that does not appear in CN/CQD (Fig. S1a and Table S2) [37,38]. This peak could be ascribed to the Bi-O bonding, which originated from in the crystal structure of [Bi2O2]2+ [39].

The high-resolution peaks of C1s in Fig. 5d compromise four constituents, including CC bond at 284.8 eV, CO bonding at 286.1, CO bonding at 288.4 eV, and sp3-bonded CN at 289.5 [40]. Compared to the CN/CQD (Fig. S1b and Table S3), the C1s peaks at 284.8 and 288.4 eV of the composite indicate the existence of CN/CQD in CN/CQD/BiOCl0.75Br0.25 heterojunction. Furthermore, the deficiency of N-content could also be observed from the N1s spectra, as shown in Fig. 5e. Accordingly, the N1s spectra of CN/CQN/BiOCl0.75Br0.25 could be deconvoluted into three characteristic peaks. The N located at 399.05 eV corresponds to the bridging N atoms in N-(C)3 [41], while the N component sited at 400.6 eV ascribed to the amino functional groups (C–NH) [41]. The N peak centered around 402.0 eV related to the graphitic species [42]. In comparison to CN/CQD (Fig. S1c and Table S4), the CN-C group has disappeared in the CN/CQD/BiOCl0.75Br0.25 composite, indicating that nitrogen vacancies have been formed in the position of CN-C [43]. In Fig. 5f and g, the peaks at 197.35 eV (2p3/2) and 198.4 eV (2p1/2) emerge as well-known characterization of Cl, while the peaks sited at 65.72 eV and 66.77 eV related to Br 3d5/2 and Br 3d1/2.

UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy determined the light absorption capacity of the pure CN, CN/CQD, CN/CQD/BiOCl, and different proportions of halogen composites. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the photoabsorption wavelength of CN is around 460 nm. The photoabsorption range of CN was redshifted to 700 nm after coupling with CQD, indicating that CQD has settled onto CN's surface, thus influencing its optical characteristics. The increased ability of the CN/CQD to absorb visible light was attributed to the CQD's inherent optical characteristics. CQD can absorb solar radiation with longer wavelengths (>550 nm) and then excite photoluminescence with a lower wavelength (400–500 nm) [44]. Furthermore, the absorption edge of CN/CQD/BiOCl0.75Br0.25 composite occurs a notable red-shifting compared to CN/CQD/BiOCl0.5Br0.5 and CN/CQD/BiOCl0.25Br0.75. Whereas CN/CQD/BiOCl exhibit a slight red-shift. These findings demonstrated that the photoabsorption property of CN/CQD/BiOCl0.75Br0.25 composites is higher than that of CN/CQD/BiOCl, CN/CQD/BiOCl0.5Br0.5 and CN/CQD/BiOCl0.25Br0.75. This could be ascribed that the composite formed by CN/CQD and the BiOCl0.75Br0.25 interface mediates the band structure and improves the light absorption ability [45]. The optical band gap energies of the CN and BiOClxBr1-x samples were determined using the Kubelka-Munk equation based on the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra.

Where α, h, ν, A, and Eg are absorption coefficients, Planck’s constant, light frequency, proportionality constant, and band gap of the semiconductor, respectively. Accordingly, n represents the characteristics of the semiconductor transition and is either 1 (direct-gap semiconductor) or 4 (indirect-gap semiconductor) [46,47]. The Eg values for CN and BiOCl0.75Br0.25 samples were calculated by extrapolating the linear parts of the curves (Fig. S2), and the values of CN and BiOCl0.75Br0.25 are 2.48 and 2.86 eV, respectively.

The photocurrent responses and electrochemical impedance measurements (EIS) of the pure CN, CN/CQD, CN/CQD/BiOCl, and CN/CQD/BiOCl0.75Br0.25 composites were undertaken to manifest the separation efficiency of the electron-hole pairs. Compared to the CN, CN/CQD, and CN/CQD/BiOCl composites shown in Fig. 6b, the CN/CQD/BiOCl0.75Br0.25 composite had the highest transient photocurrent density. This demonstrates that the photoinduced electron-hole pairs have a lower interface resistance and a higher separation and migration efficiency [48]. On the other hand, in Fig. 6c, the EIS result reflects that the impedance arc radius of CN/CQD/BiOCl0.75Br0.25 is smaller than that of CN, CN/CQD, and CN/CQD/BiOCl under visible light. The CN/CQD/BiOCl0.75Br0.25 composite exhibit enhanced separation efficiency of the photoexcited charge carriers compared with single components [49].

The degradation studies demonstrate that the absorption ability and carrier separation efficiency of CN/CQD/BiOClxBr1-x heterojunctions are significantly improved compared to CN/CQD and CN/CQD/BiOCl heterojunctions, which may benefit the photocatalytic performance of the composites. As shown in Fig. 7 a, where C is the concentration of TC after visible light irradiation (λ > 400 nm) for t min, C0 is the initial concentration of TC. Prior to the irradiation under the visible light, 60 min dark adsorption equilibrium was carried out to eliminate the influence of adsorption on the degradation. The blank experiment demonstrated that TC could not be degraded by visible irradiation. Within 30 min, TC degradation was 36 % and 47 % for CN/CQD and CN/CQD/BiOCl, respectively. It was found that the amount of CQD affects the degrading efficiency, as shown in S3. The degradation rate was enhanced as the CQD content was increased from 0.5 to 1 wt%. However, when the CQD concentration was higher than 1 %, the shading effect of CQD affected the photocatalytic activity of composites. The synthesized CN/CQD/BiOClxBr1-x heterojunctions with × = 0.75, 0.5, and 0.25 led to approximately 84.3 %, 70.0 %, and 57.85 % of TC degradation after 30 min of illumination, respectively. As a result, the degradation efficiency of CN/CQD/BiOCl0.75Br0.25 was higher than that of CN/CQD/BiOCl. And further increase or decrease in the content of Cl and Br in CN/CQD/BiOCl0.75Br0.25 heterojunctions manifested, lowering the photocatalytic efficiency and signifying the high recombination rate.

The kinetics experiment of TC can confirm the superior photocatalytic performance of CN/CQD/BiOCl0.75Br0.25 composite. The linear plots in Fig. 7b-c indicate pseudo-first-order reaction kinetics for TC degradation, with constant rates ofCN/CQ, CN/CQD/BiOCl, CN/CQD/BiOCl0.75Br0.25, CN/CQD/BiOCl0.5Br0.5, and CN/CQD/BiOCl0.25Br0.75 being 0.0073, 0.00943, 0.03611, 0.01437, and 0.01196 min−1, respectively. This superior photocatalytic activity shows that CN could be improved by CQD and BiOCl0.75Br0.25 modification. Furthermore, the improved photocatalysis of pure CN can be explained in two ways. First, the combination of CQD and BiOClxBr1-x modifies the material, resulting in improved visible light harvesting, as shown in UV–vis DRS. Second, the formation heterojunction of CN with other semiconductors (i.e., BiOClxBr1-x) enhanced the separation of electron-hole in the composite, which increased the generation of reactive oxygen species for the degradation of TC.

The pH solution of the degradant had a remarkable impact on the photocatalytic activity of CN/CQD/BiOCl0.75Br0.25. In Fig. 7d, TC's degradation efficiency at pH 3.6, 5.8, 7.0, and 10.05 was 65.3, 83.4, 58.3, and 45.2 %, respectively. The highest degradation efficiency was achieved at pH 5.8. This is due to the generation of h+, which can combine with •O2 – to produce •OH and additional reactive oxygen species (ROS), facilitating TC's effective removal. Accordingly, as shown in Table 1 , with other CN based composite materials (e.g., CFs/g-C3N4/BiOBr [50], WO3/g-C3N4/Bi2O3 [14], carbon dots (CDs) modified MoO3/g-C3N4 [10], CoP/HCCN [51], ZnSnO3/g-C3N4 [52], Bi2O2CO3/g-C3N4 [53]), the prepared CN/CQD/BiOCl0.75Br0.25 composites showed superior performance of removing TC.
Effects of the presence of organic and inorganic anions

Humic acid (HA) is a ubiquitous macromolecular organic substance that exists widely in water. As illustrated in Fig. 8 , increasing the HA concentration from 5, 10, and 15 mg/L improves the TC removal effectiveness. A considerable enhancement was observed when HA concentration was 10 mg/L based on degradation performance. The degradation performance increased by 15 % in the presence of 15 mg/L HA compared to that without HA addition. This enhancement is mainly because HA is a photosensitizer that can form 3HA* directly, or it can generate free radicals to act on TC. Previous researchers also reported the enhanced photolysis of TC with HA addition [54,55].

Different inorganic ions in aquatic environments such as NO3 –, SO4 2-, PO4 3-, and CO3 2– were also examined. As shown in Fig. 8b–e, the addition of NO3 – promotes TC's removal, which was attributed to the formation of excited nitrate after light irradiation and the induction of free radical reaction [56]. Furthermore, the slight inhibitory effect of anions SO4 2- can be ignored on the photodegradation of TC. The simultaneous photocatalytic redox efficiency of TC by CN/CQD/BiOCl0.75Br0.25 decreases with the introduction of PO4 3- and CO3 2–. As shown in Fig. 8d, the degradation efficiency of TC on CN/CQD/BiOCl0.75Br0.25 is affected by the PO4 3-, suggesting the reduction of photocatalytic activity may be due to the competition over the active sites between the PO4 - and TC molecules. Meanwhile, the addition of CO3 2– has a significant effect on the degradation of TC. This is because CO3 – has the function of a free radical scavenger (Fig. 8e), which can consume some active free radicals [57]. Such results are beneficial to the practical application of wastewater purification.

To evaluate the efficiency of CN/CQD/BiOCl0.75Br0.25 photocatalyst on TC degradation in actual wastewater, farming fish wastewater was used. For comparison, the TC concentration was set at 10 mg/L. The characteristics of the farming fish wastewater matrices are shown in (Table. S1). The degradation rate of TC by CN/CQD/BiOCl0.75Br0.25 was 69 % after 180 min of irradiation, as shown in Fig. S4. This finding is linked to the combined effects of pH and coexisting ions, and it suggests that the developed photocatalyst is a promising option for practical application.

The ternary composite was studied from the adsorption, efficient utilization of visible light, and charge transfer viewpoint to understand the improved mechanism of the photocatalytic activities. The studies of N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms and pore size distribution on the synthesized CN, CN/CQD, CN/CQD/BiOCl, and CN/CQD/BiOCl0.75Br0.25 were carried out because a higher specific surface area is associated with better adsorption [21]. The corresponding specific surface areas, pore volumes, and average pore sizes are shown in Table S2. All the prepared samples exhibited a type IV isotherm according to the IUPAC classification, indicating the presence of mesopores structures inside the material [58]. The CN/CQD/BiOCl0.75Br0.25 shows the pores centered at 18.5 nm (Fig. 9 ). Based on the geometry of the hysteresis loops and the TEM results, it is evident that CQD presence provides a higher specific surface area, which is envisaged that may lead to higher photocatalytic efficiency [10]. As shown in Fig. S5 and its inset, the CN isotherm is non-closed and porous character but has a lower specific surface area than CN/CQD and CN/CQD/BiOCl0.75Br0.25.

Moreover, the photoluminescence (PL) spectrum was employed to appraise the separation and transfer of photogenerated carriers. A higher PL intensity indicates a higher recombination rate of photoexcited electron-hole pairs, resulting in more electrons and holes not participating in oxidation and reduction activities [59]. As shown in Fig. 10 , all CN/CQD/BiOClxBr1-x composites demonstrated a significant decrease in the photoluminescence emission intensity compared to the pure CN, indicating that CN/CQD/BiOClxBr1-x composite can effectively hinder the recombination of electron-hole pairs. The weakest photoluminescence intensity of the prepared samples was observed at CN/CQD/BiOCl0.75Br0.25, which indicates that CN/CQD/BiOCl0.75Br0.25 has the best electron-hole separation property.

To understand the plausible mechanism of photocatalytic performance in the composite, the band structures of the individual components in the composite were calculated using the following equations:

The Mulliken electronegativity (χ) of a semiconductor can also be calculated from its first ionization energy (I) and the absolute value of electron affinity (A) of the semiconductor as shown in Eqs. Where a, b, c, and d are the atomic number of the individual elements. The values of χ for CN and BiOCl0.75Br0.25 are 4.73 and 6.3 eV, respectively. Based on the band gaps of CN and BiOCl0.75Br0.25, the ECB and EVB of CN and BiOCl0.75Br0.25 were calculated to be −1.01, +1.41 eV, +0.37 and 3.23 eV versus standard hydrogen electrode (SHE), respectively. Thus, the photogenerated electrons on the CB of CN could react with oxygen to form superoxide radicals (•O2 –) because the CB potential of CN (-1.01 eV) is more negative than that of the standard redox potential (O2/•O2 –, −0.046 eV) [5]. Meanwhile, the photogenerated holes on the VB of BiOCl0.75Br0.25 could oxidize –OH/H2O to form hydroxyl radical (•OH) since the VB potential of BiOCl0.75Br0.25 (3.23 eV) is more positive than that of stander redox potential (OH–/•OH, +2.27 eV) [60]. Therefore, CN/BiOCl0.75Br0.25 promotes electron and hole separation through a Z-scheme type mechanism. Thus, the CQDs play a crucial role in the photocatalytic process. The results of the EIS and PL demonstrate that the CQDs possess unique rapid electron transfer properties that facilitate charge separation and increase the concentration of charge carriers available for photoreaction. As illustrated in Fig. 11 , electrons and holes are produced in the CN/CQD/BiOCl0.75Br0.25 under visible light. Then, the photogenerated electrons in the CB of BiOCl0.75Br0.25 are moved to the CQD due to the lower Fermi level [61]. The CQD serves as the charge transmission bridge in the middle of BOCl0.75Br0.25 and CN, moving the excited electrons to recombine with the holes in the VB of CN. Subsequently, TC molecules are reduced at CB of CN by photogenerated electrons and oxidized simultaneously at VB of BiOCl0.75Br0.25 by separated holes.

In addition, the reactive species trapping experiments were carried out. As shown in Fig. 12 , isopropanol (IPA), potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7), 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ), sodium azide (NaN3), and triethanolamine (TEOA) were used as the capture agent for hydroxyl radicals (•OH), electrons (e-), superoxide radicals (O2 •-), single oxide (1O2), and the hole (h+), respectively [62]. It was found that the TC decomposition was slightly inhibited (25 %) by the addition of K2Cr2O7 and IPA. At the same time, the degradation of TC was significantly inhibited by BQ, NaN3, and TEOA, with inhibition rates of 73.4, 72.1, and 97.8 %, respectively. These results indicated that h+ had a significant impact on the photocatalytic degradation of TC.

The stability of photocatalysts after several deterioration cycles is a critical performance criterion for practical water treatment. Meanwhile, photocatalysts' excellent recycling properties and stability can effectively cut the wastewater treatment cost and avert secondary pollution. Thus, a centrifuge was utilized to separate the photocatalysts after each set of photocatalytic degradation experiments and reused for the next run. As shown in Fig. 13 a, after four recycles, the CN/CQD/BiOCl0.75Br0.25 photocatalytic could degrade 82.3 % after 30 min of irradiation. The photocatalytic characteristic before and after the reaction was studied by FT-IR and XRD (Fig. 13b-c). The crystal and chemical compositions of CN/CQD/BiOCl0.75Br0.25 correspond to the fresh sample, which demonstrates that CN/CQD/BiOCl0.75Br0.25 composites showed excellent reusability and is a good candidate for water degradation application.

The photodegradation intermediates of TC by CN/CQD/BiOCl0.75Br0.25 were detected using the liquid chromatography-mass spectrometer (LC-MS) technique. Different intermediates were identified in the solution, and their mass spectra are included in Supplementary Table 3 and Fig. S6. The product has a mass-charge ratio (m/z) of 445, corresponding to the TC molecule. The photocatalytic degradation of TC in the presence of CN/CQD/BiOCl0.75Br0.25 resulted in the formation of m/z values of 487, 414, 392, 391, 371, 314, 279, 261, 202, 181, 149, and 113. Fig. 14 depicts three possible degradation pathways according to the literature and obtained results. In the first pathway, reactive oxygen species attracted CC bonds and decomposed into P1 (m/z = 487) through the hydroxylation process. The ring-opening, addition reaction, deamination, carbonylation, and hydroxylation processes may form product P4 (m/z = 391), indicating that C O − and − CC bonds were oxidized next. And then, P5 (371) was detected due to the continuous dehydration and methylation reactions.

In the second pathway, the TC molecule was first attacked by •O2 – leading to a P1 (414) from the detachment of hydroxyl and methyl groups, which agrees with the previous results [63]. P6 (m/z = 314) and P7 (m/z = 279) was formed after the − C C − bond broken via methylation, carboxylation, hydroxylation, and ring-opening reactions. The third possible pathway, TC molecule attacked by •O2 – undergoes a deamination reaction, the product P3 (m/z = 392) was detected due to the possibility of irrupting amido and amino groups. P8 (m/z = 261) was formed in a continuous reaction by oxidation and ring-opening. Then, P9 (m/z = 202) was produced after P8 lost two methyl groups from the carbon atom ring. Following that, alcohols and acids continued to drop off the carbocycle, and P10 (m/z = 181) was produced by addition and dihydroxylation processes. The intermediate products were oxidized continuously to form smaller molecules, including P11 (m/z = 149), aromatic compounds, and P12 (m/z = 113) [18,64]. Eventually, all products were transferred to other small molecules and mineralized into CO2 and H2O.

In order to determine the level of mineralization in the CN/CQD/BiOCl0.75Br0.25 photocatalytic process, TOC removal rates were studied (Fig. S7). The reduction of TOC was 52.1 % after 180 min of photocatalyst irradiation. These results indicated that reactive species might further decompose TC and its degradation intermediates, giving rise to the ring-opening and finally mineralization to CO2 and H2O.

T.E.S.T. software further predicts the toxicity variations of TC and degradation intermediates during the photocatalytic reaction. The acute toxicity LD50 of TC for fish is 0.25 mg/L, as indicated in Table S4 and Fig. 15 a. It is classed as “very toxic” substances by the Globally Harmonized System of Classification and Labelling Chemicals (GHS). Except for P1, P2, P3, and P6, all TC by-products had lower LD50 values, indicating lower acute toxicity. Furthermore, compared to TC, the developmental toxicity of intermediates reduces initially but increases following the generation of intermediates P2, P6, and P11 (Fig. 15b). The developmental toxicity of the subsequent degradation products was also decreased, showing that significant TC oxidation is required to eliminate the toxic intermediates. However, continual photodegradation results in increased bioaccumulation (Fig. 15c). These findings are consistent with the previous report [19,54].

Additionally, the mutagenicity of the intermediates at the first stage is “positive,” even exceeding that of TC. After prolonged oxidation, these intermediates were classified as “mutagenicity negative” substances, indicating that photodegradation considerably reduces genotoxicity. The examination of toxicity reveals that the photodegradation of TC over CN/CQD/BiOCl0.75Br0.25 reduces its toxicity and the potential risk to the aquatic water. While the toxicity of the majority of TC intermediates decreased over time, some remained toxic. Therefore, photocatalytic degradation of wastewater necessitates the utilization of longer reaction times.

In conclusion, we successfully studied CN-based ternary composite for photocatalysis of TC polluted water. Based on TEM images, the CN/CQD/BiOCl0.75Br0.25 nanocomposite revealed high purity and crystallinity with a uniform dispersion of CQD and BiOCl0.75Br0.25 nanoparticles over CN sheets. The XRD and XPS analyses confirmed the presence of CQD and BiOCl0.75Br0.25 as the dominant crystalline structure in the composite. The degradation efficiency and mineralization rate of TC reached 84.3 % and 52 %, respectively. It was found that the CN/CQD/BiOCl0.75Br0.25 photocatalyst was highly stable over pH 5 and retained its efficiency after being reused four times. Compared with those previously reported in the literature (i.e., CN/BiOCl [45], CN/BiOBr [50], and CN/CQD/BiOBr [34]), the formed composite significantly improved the visible light utilization capability by combining the multiple light scattering properties of BiOClxBr1-x with the PL up-conversion of CQD. Furthermore, the toxicity of photocatalytic TC intermediates was evaluated by T.E.S.T software. The degradation by-products exhibit less acute toxicity as compared to TC. While the developmental toxicity was reduced to the point of being a “Developmental non-toxicant.” The facile processing and structural tunability of CN/CQD/BiOClxBr1-x enable the development of highly efficient photocatalyst materials for practical use. This work will pave the way for developing new approaches to optimizing heterojunction photocatalysts.
